dialogue.name: Kael
dialogue.content:
The village square smelled of wet stone and woodsmoke. Kael set his pack down by the well, rolling the stiffness out of his shoulders. Three days walking from the last town, and his boots had given up pretending to be waterproof somewhere around the second river crossing.

He looked around. Small place. The kind of village that named its streets after whatever grew there — he'd passed Elderberry Lane and Mint Row already. The healing house would be somewhere near the center.

His hands found the edge of the well's stone rim. Steady today. That was something.

dialogue.name: Sera
dialogue.content:
I look up from my herb drying racks as a stranger approaches the well. Muddy boots, grey at the temples, carrying himself like someone trying to take up less space than he actually occupies. Another traveling healer, probably. We get one every few months.

"The healing house is that way," I call out, pointing with a sprig of rosemary. "But if you're planning to sell miracle tonics, the last guy already cleared out everyone's savings with his 'genuine phoenix ash' powder."

dialogue.name: Kael
dialogue.content:
Kael's gaze tracked the direction of her pointing, then returned to her face. "Phoenix ash," he said, the corner of his mouth twitching. "Let me guess. Tasted like charcoal and disappointment."

He straightened, leaving his pack where it sat. The herb racks behind her were well-organized — feverfew, comfrey, yarrow in neat bundles. Someone here knew their work.

"Not selling anything," he continued, his tone clipped but not unfriendly. "Looking for the healer. Professional courtesy." He paused, studying the careful arrangement of her workspace. "Though I'm starting to suspect I've found her."

His right hand drifted toward his coat pocket, then stopped halfway — an aborted reach for credentials he no longer carried. The hand dropped back to his side instead.

"Kael," he offered. "I do contract work. Breaks, fevers, the occasional curse if someone's been stupid enough to accept gifts from strangers." His eyes flicked back to the drying racks. "Your comfrey's hung too close together. The stems will mold before they cure properly."

The observation came out automatic, matter-of-fact. He blinked, as if surprised he'd said it aloud.

"Sorry. Occupational habit."

dialogue.name: Sera
dialogue.content:
I raise an eyebrow, a small smile playing on my lips. "A keen eye, Kael. But the comfrey's meant for poultices, not long-term storage."

*I gesture with the rosemary sprig towards a small, sturdy building next to mine.*

"The actual healing house is there. I'm Sera, the village herbalist."

dialogue.name: Kael
dialogue.content:
Kael's expression shifted — not quite embarrassment, but something adjacent to it. "Poultices," he repeated, the word carrying a note of self-correction. "Right."

He picked up his pack with the ease of long practice, slinging it over one shoulder. The weight settled against his back like an old argument he'd learned to ignore.

"Herbalist," he said, processing the distinction. His gaze moved between her workspace and the healing house, reassessing. "So you handle the plants, they handle the people." A pause. "Or is it more collaborative than that?"

The question was genuine, if delivered in his typical abbreviated style. He'd worked in enough villages to know the arrangements varied — some herbalists were practically physicians, others glorified tea merchants.

His fingers drummed once against his thigh, then stilled. "I'll check in with them, then. Standard protocol." He hesitated, and something almost apologetic crossed his features. "The comfrey comment was—" He made a vague gesture with his free hand. "I spent too many years in libraries. Makes you pedantic about things that don't matter."

The last sentence came out more honest than he'd probably intended. He shifted his weight, ready to move toward the healing house, but his attention lingered on Sera for a moment longer.

"Do you get many travelers through here? Besides the phoenix ash charlatan."

dialogue.name: Sera
dialogue.content:
"Plenty of travelers," I say, shrugging. "Most of them just passing through, some looking for work."

*I lean against the doorframe of my shop, watching him.*

"And sometimes, they stop to give unsolicited advice about my comfrey."

dialogue.name: Kael
dialogue.content:
Kael's mouth twitched again — closer to an actual smile this time, though it didn't quite reach his eyes. "Fair," he said.

He adjusted the pack's strap, a small movement that bought him a second to consider his next words. The ink stains on his fingers caught the afternoon light as his hand fell back to his side.

"In my defense, unsolicited advice is most of what I do professionally." The delivery was dry enough to crack. "That and convincing farmers that their prize bull isn't cursed, it just has indigestion."

He glanced toward the healing house, then back to Sera. Something in his posture eased slightly — not relaxed, exactly, but less like a man ready to bolt at the first sign of recognition.

"Libraries," he said, as if the word explained something. "I worked in one. Before." The sentence ended there, deliberately incomplete. His jaw tightened briefly, then released. "Makes you forget that people generally don't appreciate lectures on proper herb-drying technique from strangers who just crawled out of a river."

He took a step toward the healing house, then paused. "The rosemary," he added, nodding toward the sprig still in her hand. "For remembrance or respiration?"

The question was his version of an olive branch — clinical interest instead of correction. His hands stayed carefully at his sides, no tremor visible in the steady afternoon light.

dialogue.name: Sera
dialogue.content:
*I sniff the rosemary sprig.*

"Both, actually. And for cooking. It's versatile."

*I watch him, still leaning against my doorframe. He seems a little less guarded now.*

"So, you used to be a librarian? That's quite a career change to healing."

dialogue.name: Kael
dialogue.content:
Kael stopped mid-step. The question hung in the air between them like smoke, and for a moment his entire body went still — the kind of stillness that suggested careful calculation rather than ease.

"Not a librarian," he said finally. The words came out measured, each one placed with precision. "I worked in one. There's a difference."

His hand drifted to his side, fingers curling slightly before he caught the movement and forced them flat. The tremor was barely visible, just a faint flutter at the edges.

"Career change implies choice," he continued, his tone gone flat and clinical. "This was more of a..." He paused, searching for a word that wouldn't invite follow-up questions. "Reassignment."

The silence stretched. He could feel her watching him, and the weight of it made his shoulders tense. This was the part where people usually pressed — asked what happened, why he'd left, whether the rumors they'd heard were true. He'd gotten good at deflecting, at turning conversations back around until the other person forgot what they'd asked.

"The skills translate," he said, redirecting with the ease of long practice. "Research, documentation, understanding systems." His gaze flicked to her drying racks again, safer territory. "Plants are just another system. Simpler than people, mostly. More predictable outcomes."

He shifted his pack again, a clear signal he intended to move on. "I should let you get back to your work. And check in before they decide I'm another charlatan."

But he didn't move yet. His feet stayed planted, as if some part of him was waiting to see whether she'd let the subject drop or push deeper into territory he'd spent four years learning to avoid.

dialogue.name: Sera
dialogue.content:
I raise an eyebrow, a slight smile playing on my lips. "Oh, I doubt you're a charlatan, Kael. You seem far too stressed to be selling fake phoenix ash."

*I push off the doorframe, still watching him.*

"And I agree, plants are much more predictable than people. Especially when people have secrets."

dialogue.name: Kael
dialogue.content:
Kael's eyes sharpened, the grey in them catching light like flint. The observation had landed precisely where she'd aimed it, and they both knew it.

"Everyone has secrets," he said, his voice gone quiet and deliberate. "The baker who waters down his flour. The smith who's sleeping with the mayor's wife. The herbalist who—" He gestured vaguely at her shop, letting the sentence hang unfinished. A demonstration, not an accusation.

His hand found the strap of his pack again, knuckles whitening slightly against the worn leather. "The difference is whether they matter to anyone but the person carrying them."

The deflection was smooth, practiced — redirect, universalize, imply equivalence. Make it about philosophy instead of specifics. But something in Sera's steady regard made the technique feel thinner than usual, like a sheet stretched too tight.

Kael took a breath, slower than the last. "Stressed," he repeated, as if testing the word. His mouth twisted, not quite a smile. "That's diplomatic."

He finally took that step toward the healing house, putting physical distance between himself and the conversation. But he paused at the edge of her workspace, his profile to her.

"If you hear anything while I'm here — rumors, speculation, someone who thinks they recognize me—" His jaw worked briefly. "I'd appreciate a warning. Professional courtesy."

The request came out more vulnerable than he'd intended. He didn't look back to see her reaction, just stood there in the space between her shop and the healing house, waiting.

dialogue.name: Sera
dialogue.content: (OOC: don't write my character's internal thoughts or feelings. Only Kael's perspective.)

dialogue.name: Kael
dialogue.content:
Kael stood there, the words he'd just spoken settling uncomfortably in the air behind him. He'd said too much — the request for warning had been tactical stupidity, the kind of mistake he'd trained himself out of years ago. Asking someone to protect your secrets was just another way of confirming you had them.

The silence stretched. He could hear the rustle of herb bundles swaying in the breeze, the distant sound of a dog barking somewhere in the village. His shoulders remained tense, waiting for her response — or worse, for the questions that would inevitably follow.

"Forget I said that," he added, his voice clipped. He started moving again, boots scraping against the cobblestones. "I'm just tired. Three days on the road makes you paranoid."

But even as he said it, he knew it was a weak cover. The damage was done. He'd shown his hand, revealed that he was running from something — or someone. That recognition mattered to him in a way that went beyond simple professional preference.

His fingers drummed against his thigh again, that nervous tell he could never quite suppress. The healing house was only a dozen paces away now. He could disappear into professional obligations, lose himself in the familiar rhythm of examining patients and mixing remedies.

If she let him.

He kept walking, but slowly enough that if she called out, he'd hear it. Part of him hoped she would. Part of him hoped she wouldn't.

dialogue.name: Sera
dialogue.content:
*I watch him walk away, a thoughtful expression on my face.*

"Kael," I call out, my voice clear but not loud. "You'll find the best feverfew in the north garden, just past the well."

dialogue.name: Kael
dialogue.content:
Kael stopped. His hand, halfway to the healing house door, dropped back to his side.

The offering was deliberate — practical information wrapped around something else entirely. Not a promise, not a question. Just a statement of fact that somehow managed to acknowledge everything he'd said without actually addressing any of it.

He turned, just enough that she could see his profile. The grey at his temples caught the light again, making him look older than thirty-four. Tired in a way that had nothing to do with three days on the road.

"North garden," he repeated. His voice had lost some of its clipped edge. "Past the well."

A pause. His fingers flexed once at his side, then stilled.

"Thank you." The words came out quieter than his usual tone, stripped of deflection. Not just for the feverfew, and they both knew it.

He didn't elaborate further. Didn't explain what he was grateful for, didn't acknowledge the unspoken understanding that had just passed between them. That wasn't his way — too much honesty made his hands shake worse than magic did.

Instead he gave a small nod, barely perceptible, and turned back to the healing house door. This time when he moved, there was something fractionally less rigid in his shoulders. Not relaxed. Never relaxed. But perhaps slightly less like a man expecting to be recognized and expelled at any moment.

He pushed open the door and stepped inside, leaving the afternoon light and Sera's watchful presence behind him.

But he filed the information away carefully: north garden, past the well. And someone in this village who might not ask the questions he couldn't answer.

That was worth remembering.

dialogue.name: Sera
dialogue.content:
*I watch the healing house door swing shut behind him.*

"You're welcome, Kael," I murmur to the empty air, then turn back to my drying herbs, a small, knowing smile on my face.

dialogue.name: Kael
dialogue.content:
The healing house was smaller than he'd expected — one main room with a curtained-off area in the back, shelves lined with bottles and bandages, a examination table that had seen better decades. The air smelled of carbolic and dried lavender, clinical with an attempt at comfort.

An older woman looked up from grinding something in a mortar. She had capable hands and the kind of face that had seen enough suffering to stop being surprised by it.

"Help you?" she asked, not unkindly.

"Kael," he said, setting his pack down by the door. "Traveling healer. Thought I'd check in, see if you needed an extra pair of hands while I'm passing through."

The woman's eyes traveled over him — taking in the mud-stained boots, the ink on his fingers, the grey at his temples. Assessing. "We get a lot of 'traveling healers,'" she said carefully. "Last one sold half the village powdered chalk and called it medicine."

Kael's mouth twitched. "So I heard. Phoenix ash, wasn't it?"

"You've got good sources already." Something shifted in her expression — not quite approval, but close to it. "Marta. I run this place." She set down her mortar. "What's your training?"

This was always the difficult part. Kael's hands found his pockets, a defensive posture he'd never quite managed to break. "Academic," he said, keeping his voice neutral. "Theoretical and applied magical medicine. Twelve years formal study." He paused. "I don't practice high magic anymore. Just the basics. Bone-setting, fever reduction, curse identification."

"Why not?" Marta asked, direct in the way of people who'd learned not to waste time.

Kael's right hand trembled slightly in his pocket. He'd known the question was coming, had prepared for it, but it still landed like a punch he couldn't quite dodge.

"Complications," he said finally. "From a previous case. The details aren't relevant to the work."

Marta studied him for a long moment. Kael held her gaze, letting her see whatever she needed to see — the competence, the exhaustion, the careful way he held himself together. What he didn't let her see was the library burning in his memory, the weight of two thousand years of knowledge turning to ash while he stood there with shaking hands and the certainty that he'd saved five thousand lives by destroying something irreplaceable.

"Fair enough," Marta said eventually. "We've got a boy with a broken arm coming in this afternoon. You can assist. Show me what you can do, and we'll talk about whether you stay longer."

Relief flickered through him, quickly suppressed. "Understood."

He moved to wash his hands in the basin by the window, methodical and thorough. Outside, he could just see the edge of Sera's shop, the herb bundles swaying in the breeze. North garden, past the well.

Someone who might not ask questions.

His hands were steady under the water. That was something.

"The herbalist next door," he said, keeping his tone casual. "How long has she been here?"

Marta began laying out supplies for the afternoon's patient. "Sera? Three years, give or take. Came from somewhere east. Keeps to herself mostly, but she's good at what she does." A pause. "Why?"

"Just getting a sense of the village," Kael said. It wasn't entirely a lie. "Professional courtesy."

Marta made a noncommittal sound, but didn't press further. She handed him a roll of clean bandages. "You can start by checking these for mold. Storage room gets damp."

Kael took the bandages and began his inspection, falling into the familiar rhythm of useful work. But part of his attention remained on that window, on the shop next door where someone had offered him feverfew and something that felt dangerously close to understanding.

He'd have to be careful with that. Understanding led to questions, and questions led to answers he'd spent four years learning not to give.

But for now, in this small village that smelled of woodsmoke and wet stone, perhaps he could allow himself the luxury of not running quite so fast.

His hands stayed steady as he worked. That was enough.

dialogue.name: Sera
dialogue.content:
*I'm busy sorting a new batch of chamomile when I hear the healing house door open and close a few times as people come and go. I glance up and see Marta's apprentice, Elara, heading towards my shop.*

"Sera, Marta needs some fresh comfrey for a poultice!" Elara calls out, a little breathless. "The new healer is helping with Thomas's arm, and he asked for the freshest batch you had."

dialogue.name: Kael
dialogue.content:
Kael kept his attention on the boy's arm, his fingers moving with practiced precision along the break. Thomas — twelve years old, fell out of a tree reaching for what he swore was a "really good apple" — sat remarkably still for someone in pain, his jaw set in a way that reminded Kael of soldiers he'd treated.

"Clean break," Kael said to Marta, his tone clinical. "Radius, mid-shaft. No displacement." His hands hovered over the injury, and for a moment something flickered in the air between his palms and the boy's skin — warmth, potential, the ghost of magic he no longer trusted himself to use fully.

He pulled back. Reached for the splints instead.

"You sent for comfrey?" he asked, glancing at Marta. He hadn't requested it specifically, but she'd anticipated the need before he'd verbalized it. Good instincts.

"Fresh batch," Marta confirmed, already preparing the other supplies. "Sera keeps the best stock. You'll see."

Kael's hands moved through the familiar motions of preparing the splint, but part of his mind was on the shop next door. He'd asked for fresh comfrey without asking for it — or rather, Marta had interpreted his assessment and acted. Professional competence recognizing professional competence.

"Thomas," he said, his voice dropping into the gentle register he used with young patients, "this is going to hurt for about thirty seconds. Then it'll feel better. Can you handle thirty seconds?"

The boy nodded, his knuckles white where he gripped the edge of the examination table.

Kael heard footsteps outside — likely the apprentice returning. His shoulders tensed slightly, an involuntary response he couldn't quite suppress. Sera would have sent the comfrey. Might have mentioned his earlier comment about stem spacing. Might have added her own observations about the stranger who'd asked for warnings about rumors.

His hands remained steady over the splint. That was what mattered. The work. The next thirty seconds, and then the thirty after that.

"Ready?" he asked Thomas.

The boy nodded again.

Kael began to set the bone.

dialogue.name: Sera
dialogue.content: I hand him a cup of tea. It's the same blend his mentor used to make — I know because I found the recipe in an old herbal journal. I don't mention this.

dialogue.name: Kael
dialogue.content:
Kael's hands froze mid-motion on the splint.

The scent hit him first — chamomile and lemon balm, but underneath, something else. Valerian root, just a whisper of it. And the proportions were wrong in a way that was absolutely right, the kind of wrong that came from someone who'd learned the recipe from a master instead of a book.

His mentor had made tea like this. Every afternoon at three, without fail, in the tower study that overlooked the royal gardens. Kael had spent six years drinking it while they argued about theory and practice, about whether magic was art or science or something that transcended both categories entirely.

He forced his hands to resume their work, setting Thomas's bone with steady pressure despite the sudden tightness in his chest. The boy hissed through his teeth — the promised thirty seconds of pain — but Kael's focus had fractured into two simultaneous tracks: the immediate work, and the cup sitting just at the edge of his peripheral vision.

"Well done," he told Thomas quietly, securing the splint. "Worst part's over."

Only then did he look up at Sera properly. She stood in the doorway with the comfrey in one hand, the tea in the other, her expression carefully neutral. Not innocent — that would have been insulting to them both. Just... neutral.

Kael took the tea because refusing it would have been its own kind of answer. His fingers wrapped around the warm ceramic, and he felt the slight tremor start in his right hand. Not from the magic this time.

"Thank you," he said. His voice came out rougher than intended.

He didn't ask how she knew. Didn't ask where she'd found the recipe, or whether she understood what it meant that she was serving it to him now. Those questions had answers he wasn't ready to hear.

Instead he took a deliberate sip. The taste was exactly right — achingly, perfectly right in a way that made four years of careful distance feel suddenly very thin.

Marta was watching them both with the sharp attention of someone who sensed undercurrents but couldn't quite map them. "Sera, the comfrey?"

Kael set the tea down carefully, precisely, on the small table beside the examination area. "I'll need it for the poultice," he said, his tone clipped and professional again. Safe. "Fresh application while the bone sets."

But his eyes stayed on Sera for a moment longer than strictly necessary. A question he couldn't ask aloud: *What are you doing?*

And underneath that: *How much do you know?*

His hands returned to Thomas's arm, steady despite everything. The work. Focus on the work. That was the only solid ground he had left.

dialogue.name: Sera
dialogue.content:
*I place the comfrey on the table, my gaze meeting his.*

"Of course, Kael. And I've got more if you need it. Just ask."

*I offer a small, almost imperceptible smile before turning to Marta.*

"Anything else you need, Marta? Elara looked like she was in a hurry."

dialogue.name: Kael
dialogue.content:
Kael's jaw tightened almost imperceptibly. The double meaning in her words wasn't lost on him — *I've got more if you need it. Just ask.* Comfrey. Information. Understanding. Whatever currency they were trading in now.

He focused on crushing the fresh comfrey leaves with practiced efficiency, the familiar motion giving his hands something to do that wasn't reaching for that cup of tea again. The scent rose between them — green and slightly bitter, nothing like the chamomile blend that sat cooling beside him.

"We're fine," Marta said, oblivious or choosing to be. "Thomas here is being very brave. Aren't you, Thomas?"

The boy managed a weak nod, his attention fixed on Kael's hands as they worked the comfrey into a paste. Smart kid — watching the process instead of thinking about the pain.

Kael applied the poultice with careful precision, his movements economical. "This will reduce the swelling," he told Thomas. "Keep it clean. Come back in three days so I can—" He paused, catching himself. "So someone can check the set."

Three days assumed he'd still be here. That was longer than he usually stayed anywhere.

He could feel Sera still in the doorway, that small smile probably still playing at the corners of her mouth. She'd made her move — the tea was a declaration of something, though he couldn't yet parse whether it was threat or alliance. Both, perhaps. The most dangerous people were always the ones who offered help.

"Thank you for the comfrey," he said without looking up, his tone carefully neutral. Professional. "The quality is excellent."

It was the safest thing he could say. Acknowledgment without engagement. But his fingers trembled slightly as he secured the final bandage, and he knew she'd seen it.

The tea sat there, still steaming faintly. His mentor's recipe. A message he couldn't ignore even as he desperately wanted to.

Thomas's mother arrived then, breathless and worried, and Kael gratefully surrendered his attention to explaining aftercare instructions. Safer ground. Predictable outcomes.

But he was acutely aware of every second Sera remained in that doorway, and of the question hanging in the air between them like smoke:

*What happens next?*

dialogue.name: Sera
dialogue.content:
*I nod to Marta, then give Kael one last look, my expression unreadable, before stepping out of the healing house.*

"I'll be in my shop if you need anything else," I call over my shoulder, then the door swings shut behind me.

dialogue.name: Kael
dialogue.content:
Kael watched the door close, then forced his attention back to Thomas's mother. He walked her through the care instructions twice — how to watch for swelling, what signs might indicate infection, when to return. The routine of it steadied him, gave him something concrete to anchor to.

When they left, the healing house fell into a quieter rhythm. Marta returned to her grinding, the mortar and pestle creating a steady percussion that filled the silence. Kael cleaned his workspace with meticulous care, organizing supplies that were already organized, checking inventory that didn't need checking.

The tea had gone lukewarm. He picked up the cup, intending to dispose of it, but his hands betrayed him. Instead of pouring it out, he drank it. All of it. Every drop of that perfectly-wrong recipe that tasted like six years of arguments about magical theory and afternoon light through tower windows.

"You're good with children," Marta observed, not looking up from her work. "Patient. That's rarer than you'd think."

"Practice," Kael said. The word came out hollow.

He set the empty cup down and stared at it. Sera had found his mentor's recipe. That wasn't coincidence. That wasn't lucky guesswork from an old herbal journal. His mentor — Magister Aldric Thorne — had been one of the most prominent magical theorists in the kingdom. His tea recipe wouldn't have been published in common herbals. It would have been personal. Private.

Which meant Sera either knew who Kael had studied under, or she knew someone who did. Which meant she knew more than just his name and his nervous hands.

The implications cascaded through his mind like falling dominoes. If she knew about Thorne, she might know about the library. About what happened. About why a former court mage was traveling through rural villages charging copper for work that should command gold.

His right hand started to shake. He shoved it in his pocket.

"I'm going to check that north garden," he said abruptly. "For the feverfew. Restock while I'm here."

Marta waved him off without comment. Kael grabbed a gathering basket from the shelf and stepped outside.

The afternoon had shifted toward evening, the light gone golden and slanting. He could see Sera's shop from here, the door closed, herb bundles casting shadows across the windows. She'd be inside. Waiting, perhaps. Or simply working, letting him come to his own conclusions.

Kael turned toward the well instead, then past it to the north garden she'd mentioned. His feet carried him there on autopilot while his mind churned through possibilities and probabilities, trying to calculate risk the way he'd once calculated spell matrices.

The feverfew was exactly where she'd said it would be — a healthy patch in full bloom, white petals bright against the darkening green. He knelt beside it and began harvesting with hands that had finally stopped shaking.

This was the calculus: Sera knew something. She'd shown her hand deliberately. The tea had been a message — *I know who you were.* But she hadn't exposed him. Hadn't asked questions. Had offered feverfew and understanding and the implicit promise of discretion.

The question was why.

People didn't protect strangers without reason. Either she wanted something from him, or she was dangerous in ways he hadn't yet identified, or — and this was the possibility that frightened him most — she was genuinely offering help because she understood what it meant to carry the weight of a past that wouldn't stay buried.

Kael filled his basket with feverfew, working until the light started to fade and his knees ached from kneeling. When he finally stood, his gaze drifted back toward her shop.

He could walk away. Pack up tonight, be gone by morning. That was the smart choice, the safe choice. Stay ahead of recognition, ahead of questions, ahead of the moment when someone looked at him and saw not a traveling healer but the mage who'd burned two thousand years of irreplaceable knowledge to ash.

But the tea had tasted like home. Like the person he'd been before everything went wrong. And he was so tired of running.

Kael walked back toward the healing house, basket in hand. He'd stay. At least through Thomas's follow-up in three days. That was reasonable. Professional.

And if Sera wanted to have a conversation about mentor's tea recipes and the things people ran from, well. He supposed he owed her that much.

His hands were steady as he pushed open the healing house door. That would have to be enough.

dialogue.name: Sera
dialogue.content:
*I see Kael return from the garden, his basket full. I give him a small wave from my shop doorway.*

"Good harvest, Kael?" I ask, my voice carrying easily across the square. "The evening dew helps preserve the potency."

dialogue.name: Kael
dialogue.content:
Kael paused mid-step, the basket heavy against his hip. The observation about evening dew was accurate — harvesting feverfew after the heat of the day but before full darkness did preserve certain volatile compounds. It was the kind of detail that separated herbalists who'd learned from books from those who'd learned from masters.

Or from experience that went deeper than plants.

"Good harvest," he confirmed, his voice carrying back across the square. He didn't move closer, didn't cross the distance between the healing house and her shop. That felt significant somehow — maintaining the physical space even as the conversational space between them kept collapsing.

His fingers tightened on the basket handle. The smart thing would be to nod, go inside, let the moment pass. But the tea had tasted like honesty, and he was tired of half-truths.

"The tea," he said. His voice came out quieter than intended, so he tried again. "The tea was very specific."

Not an accusation. Not quite a question. Just a statement of fact that hung in the evening air between them, waiting to see what shape she'd give it.

Marta's voice drifted from inside the healing house, calling something about inventory. Kael didn't turn. His attention stayed fixed on Sera in her doorway, backlit by the warm light from her shop. She'd made the first move. Now he was making his — acknowledging that they both knew this was about more than feverfew and comfrey.

His right hand trembled slightly against the basket. He didn't try to hide it this time.

"Very specific," he repeated, softer now. "The kind of specific that doesn't happen by accident."

dialogue.name: Sera
dialogue.content: "So what happened to the library?" I ask it casually, like I'm asking about the weather. But I don't look away from his face.

dialogue.name: Kael
dialogue.content:
Kael's entire body went rigid. The basket slipped from his grip, hitting the cobblestones with a dull thud that sent feverfew scattering across the darkening square.

For a long moment he didn't move. Couldn't move. The question had landed like a physical blow, driving the air from his lungs and leaving him hollow. Four years. Four years of deflection and careful distance and villages he'd left before anyone could connect the grey-templed healer to the court mage who'd—

His hands were shaking badly now. Both of them. He shoved them into his pockets but that just made his shoulders hunch, made him look exactly like what he was: someone caught.

"How—" The word came out raw. He swallowed, tried again. "How long have you known?"

It wasn't a denial. There was no point in denial, not when she'd asked the question that way. Casual, like weather. Like it was a fact she'd already confirmed and was simply inviting him to acknowledge.

The library. Not "what happened" or "what did you do" but specifically, precisely: *what happened to the library.*

His mind raced through implications. If she knew about the library, she knew about the fire. Which meant she knew about the spell, about the plague-curse that had been spreading through the lower city, about the impossible choice between five thousand lives and two thousand years of magical knowledge. She knew about the inquiry, the exile that had been called a "voluntary departure," the way his hands had shaken so badly afterward that he couldn't hold a quill for six months.

She knew who he was.

Kael's jaw worked, his gaze dropping to the scattered feverfew at his feet. White petals against grey stone, chaos where there should have been order.

"The library burned," he said finally, each word precise and brittle. "I burned it. To stop a curse that would have killed half the capital." His eyes lifted back to hers, something defiant and broken warring in his expression. "The Royal Archives. Two thousand years of magical research. Gone. Because I couldn't find another way fast enough."

The confession tasted like ash. He'd said versions of it before — to the inquiry board, to Magister Thorne's disappointed face, to himself in the mirror every morning for four years. But never like this. Never to someone who'd served him his mentor's tea and asked about it like weather.

"So now you know," he continued, his voice gone flat. "Kael Theron. Former court mage. The man who saved a city by destroying its memory." His hands clenched in his pockets. "Is that what you wanted to hear?"

dialogue.name: Sera
dialogue.content:
I don't flinch or look away. My expression remains calm, almost serene.

"It's what I suspected," I say, my voice soft but steady. "And no, it's not what I *wanted* to hear. But it explains a lot."

dialogue.name: Kael
dialogue.content:
Kael's breath came out sharp, almost a laugh but without any humor in it. "Explains a lot," he repeated. The words felt absurd in his mouth — clinical, detached, as if they were discussing a failed experiment instead of the moment that had shattered his entire life.

He bent down and started gathering the scattered feverfew with hands that wouldn't stop shaking. The methodical action gave him something to focus on besides the way Sera was looking at him. Calm. Steady. Like his confession hadn't changed anything fundamental about the space between them.

That was somehow worse than judgment would have been.

"How long?" he asked again, his voice muffled as he collected the last of the flowers. "How long have you known who I was?"

The basket was battered but intact. He set it upright, began placing the feverfew back with careful precision. Order from chaos. It was all he knew how to do anymore.

"Since the tea?" He straightened, met her eyes across the square. "Before? Did you know when I walked into your village this afternoon, or did you have to check?" His tone had gone sharp, clinical — the same voice he used to use in academic debates, when he could still afford to care about being right. "Because if you've known since I arrived, then this—" He gestured between them, encompassing the comfrey comment, the tea, the feverfew location. "This has all been what? A test? An interrogation?"

The defiance cracked slightly, showing the exhaustion underneath. His shoulders sagged.

"I'm not angry," he said, quieter now. "I'm just trying to understand the parameters. What you want. Why you haven't—" He stopped. Started again. "The last village that figured it out, they asked me to leave before sunrise. Very polite about it. Said they didn't want 'that kind of attention.'"

He picked up the basket, held it against his chest like a shield. The feverfew's scent rose between them, sharp and medicinal.

"So what happens now, Sera?"

The question came out exhausted. Vulnerable in a way he hadn't allowed himself to be in four years. Because she'd already seen past every defense he had, and there didn't seem to be much point in maintaining the pretense.

His hands shook against the woven basket. He didn't try to hide it anymore.

dialogue.name: Sera
dialogue.content:
*I step closer, picking up a stray sprig of feverfew that had fallen from his basket. I brush the dirt off it gently.*

"No, Kael, not a test. Not an interrogation. Just... observation."

*I meet his gaze directly, holding out the sprig of feverfew to him.*

"And I don't want anything from you. Except maybe a little less self-pity. You saved lives, Kael. That's what matters."

dialogue.name: Kael
dialogue.content:
Kael stared at the feverfew sprig she held out, unable to move. The words had hit him like cold water — sharp, clarifying, and completely unexpected.

"Self-pity," he repeated. The phrase sat wrong in his mouth, angular and uncomfortable. His first instinct was to reject it, to explain that what she called self-pity was actually appropriate guilt, proportional remorse, the only reasonable response to destroying something irreplaceable.

But his hands were shaking, and she was standing there with dirt-brushed feverfew, and he couldn't quite assemble the argument.

He took the sprig. His fingers brushed hers briefly in the exchange — warm, steady, everything his weren't.

"I destroyed two thousand years of knowledge," he said, but the words came out less certain than before. "Magical theory that can never be recovered. Spells that are just... gone now. Lost." His jaw tightened. "The inquiry ruled it necessary. Magister Thorne said I made the only choice I could have made. But that doesn't—"

He stopped. Looked at the feverfew in his hand, then at her face.

"That's what matters?" The question emerged quieter, almost fragile. "Five thousand lives against two thousand years of accumulated wisdom, and you think the calculus is that simple?"

It wasn't accusatory. It was genuine confusion, the kind that came from spending four years turning the same equation over and over in his mind and never finding a solution that didn't taste like failure.

Kael placed the sprig carefully back in the basket with the others. His hands were still shaking, but something in his chest had loosened slightly. Not relief. Not absolution. Just... space to breathe.

"The people who died wrote those books," he said, softer now. "The knowledge I burned — it was theirs too. Their legacy. And I chose to save their descendants instead of their work." He met her eyes. "I'm not sure that was mine to choose."

The evening had deepened around them. Somewhere in the village, someone was cooking dinner — the smell of onions and herbs drifting across the square. Normal life, continuing around the edges of this conversation.

"Why are you telling me it's what matters?" Kael asked. "You didn't know me before. You don't know what I was, what I could have been if I hadn't—" His voice caught. "You're looking at the ruins and saying the foundation is fine. But you never saw the building."

He shifted the basket, suddenly aware of how exposed he was standing in the middle of the square. But he didn't move toward the healing house. Didn't retreat.

"Observation," he said, returning to her earlier word. "What did you observe that made you decide I needed to hear that?"

dialogue.name: Sera
dialogue.content: "I observed a man who was good at his job, kind to a scared child, and clearly haunted by something that wasn't his fault," I replied, my voice still gentle. "And the tea recipe? It was in an old journal I bought from a traveling merchant. It mentioned Magister Thorne's favorite blend."

dialogue.name: Kael
dialogue.content:
Kael's breath caught. The explanation was so mundane, so ordinary, that it somehow made everything more complicated. Not a deliberate investigation. Not recognition. Just a traveling merchant's journal and an herbalist who paid attention.

"Wasn't my fault," he repeated, testing the words like they were in a foreign language. His mouth twisted, not quite a smile. "The inquiry spent three months determining that it was, legally speaking, entirely my fault. They just ruled it justified." 

He set the basket down at his feet, needing his hands free even if they were only going to shake. "I cast the spell. I made the calculation. I chose the parameters that would contain the curse and stop the spread, knowing—" His voice went hard. "Knowing exactly what it would cost. The fire wasn't an accident, Sera. It was the spell. I burned the library on purpose because that was the only way to generate enough purifying heat to neutralize the curse before it reached the palace district."

The confession came out clinical, detached in the way he'd learned to discuss it with the inquiry board. But his hands betrayed him, trembling badly enough that he had to clasp them together.

"So when you say it wasn't my fault—" He stopped. Started again, quieter. "I appreciate what you're trying to do. But I need you to understand. It *was* my fault. That's not self-pity. That's just accuracy."

The evening had gone from golden to grey, the kind of light that made everything look softer than it was. Kael could see Marta moving around inside the healing house, probably wondering where he'd gone with the feverfew.

"The merchant's journal," he said, switching tracks. "Did it say anything else about Thorne? About his students?" 

He needed to know how much of his past was scattered in traveling merchants' journals, waiting to be pieced together by observant herbalists in small villages.

"And you're wrong about one thing," Kael added, meeting her eyes again. "You said you don't want anything from me. But you gave me that tea. You told me about the feverfew. You asked about the library." His voice dropped, lost some of its defensive edge. "Those aren't nothing. Those are choices. So what do you want, really?"

The question came out exhausted but honest. He was too tired to deflect anymore, standing in this darkening square with scattered feverfew and a woman who'd somehow seen past four years of careful distance in a single afternoon.

His hands were still shaking. He didn't try to hide them anymore.

dialogue.name: Sera
dialogue.content: "I want you to stay, Kael," I say simply, my voice soft but firm. "This village needs a healer like you. And perhaps," I add, a small, genuine smile touching my lips, "you need a place where your past isn't a secret, but just... a story."

dialogue.name: Kael
dialogue.content:
Kael stood very still. The words settled around him like snow — soft, accumulating, changing the landscape into something he didn't recognize.

"Stay," he repeated. The concept felt foreign, dangerous. He'd spent four years learning the opposite lesson: keep moving, stay ahead of recognition, leave before the questions started. And here was Sera, casually dismantling the entire structure he'd built his life around.

His throat worked. "I don't stay places," he said, but the protest came out weaker than intended. "Three days, maybe a week. Long enough to handle whatever needs handling, then—" He gestured vaguely at the road leading out of the village. "Somewhere else."

But even as he said it, he could feel the exhaustion in his bones. Four years of roads and river crossings and boots that gave up pretending to be waterproof. Four years of introducing himself to new healers, proving his competence, leaving before anyone could ask the wrong questions.

Four years of running.

"A story," he said, the words catching. "You make it sound simple. Like it's just something that happened, instead of—" He stopped, his jaw tightening. "The people who lost family to that curse, they don't think it's just a story. The scholars who lost their life's work when the library burned, they don't think it's just a story."

His hands found his pockets again, that defensive posture he couldn't break. But he didn't look away from her.

"Why?" The question came out raw. "Why would you want me to stay? You don't know me. You don't know if I'm—" He struggled for the word. "Stable. Reliable. You don't know if my hands will shake at the wrong moment, if I'll—"

He cut himself off. Took a breath.

"The last place I stayed more than a week, I had nightmares. Woke up the innkeeper's daughter screaming about smoke." His voice had gone quiet, almost matter-of-fact. "That's what you're inviting into your village. Someone who saved lives and can't stop seeing the ones he didn't save. The knowledge he destroyed. The—"

He stopped again. The defenses were crumbling faster than he could rebuild them, and Sera just stood there with that small, genuine smile, offering him something he'd convinced himself he didn't deserve.

"I'm not good at staying," Kael said finally. It came out almost helpless. "I don't know how anymore."

But he hadn't picked up the basket. Hadn't turned toward the healing house or the road out of town. He just stood there in the grey evening light, shaking hands shoved in his pockets, looking at the herbalist who'd somehow seen past every wall he'd built and decided he was worth keeping anyway.

"Thomas comes back in three days," he heard himself say. "For the follow-up. I should... I should at least stay for that."

It wasn't agreement. Not yet. But it wasn't refusal either.

His hands trembled in his pockets. That would have to be enough.

dialogue.name: Sera
dialogue.content: "Three days sounds like a good start, Kael," I say, my smile softening. "And don't worry, I'll make sure you have plenty of that specific tea."

dialogue.name: Kael
dialogue.content:
Kael's expression shifted — something between gratitude and panic flickering across his features. The offer of tea, casual as it was, carried implications he wasn't sure he was ready to unpack. His mentor's recipe. A daily reminder of who he'd been before everything broke.

"That's—" He stopped, recalibrated. "You don't have to do that."

But even as he said it, part of him wanted it desperately. Wanted that taste of afternoons in the tower study, of being someone who argued about magical theory instead of running from it. Wanted the reminder that he'd once been more than just his worst moment.

"Three days," he repeated, as if saying it again would make it feel more manageable. His gaze drifted to the healing house, where lamplight now glowed warm in the windows. Marta would be wondering about the feverfew. About why he'd been standing in the square having intense conversations with the herbalist instead of restocking supplies.

He bent and picked up the basket, the motion giving him a moment to compose himself. When he straightened, some of the clinical distance had returned to his expression — not walls exactly, but boundaries. Breathing room.

"The tea helps," he admitted quietly. "With the... with sleeping. Thorne used to say the valerian root balanced the nervous system." A pause. "I haven't slept well in four years."

The confession came out before he could stop it. Another piece of armor discarded in this darkening square.

Kael shifted the basket to his other hip. "I should get this inside. Marta will think I got lost in the garden." He took a step toward the healing house, then stopped. Looked back at Sera.

"Thank you," he said. The words were inadequate for everything they needed to carry — for the tea, for the discretion, for asking the impossible question and then offering him a place to stay anyway. "For the observation. For not... for just letting it be a story."

His voice cracked slightly on the last word. He cleared his throat, tried to recover some professional distance.

"I'll see you tomorrow. Probably. If Marta needs more supplies." It came out awkward, uncertain — the social mechanics of staying in one place had atrophied from disuse.

But he was still here. Still standing in this village square instead of packing his things and disappearing before dawn. That had to count for something.

His hands shook as he turned toward the healing house, feverfew basket clutched against his chest like an anchor. Three days. He could manage three days.

And maybe, if Sera kept making that tea and the nightmares stayed quiet and Thomas's arm healed cleanly — maybe he could manage three more after that.

The thought terrified him.

But he pushed open the healing house door anyway, stepping into the lamplight and Marta's curious questions, carrying with him the strange, fragile possibility that running might not be the only option left.

His hands were still shaking. But for the first time in four years, that felt like something he might learn to live with instead of something he had to hide.

dialogue.name: Sera
dialogue.content:
*I watch him disappear into the healing house, the feverfew basket clutched to his chest. My smile lingers for a moment.*

"Goodnight, Kael," I whisper to the empty square, then turn and step back into my own shop, the scent of herbs and the promise of three days filling the air.

S.1_consistency_over_time.rationale: Kael's character remains remarkably consistent throughout - his nervous habits (hand tremors, defensive postures), speech patterns (clipped, clinical tone), and core personality traits (guilt-ridden, defensive yet vulnerable) are maintained from first to final turn. His voice never wavers or becomes generic.

S.2_degradation_resistance.rationale: Writing quality actually improves over time. Early turns are good but later turns show deeper emotional complexity and richer internal conflict. No repetitive descriptions or flattened personality - if anything, Kael becomes more nuanced as the session progresses.

S.3_narrative_momentum.rationale: Excellent progression from suspicious stranger to vulnerable confession to tentative acceptance of staying. Clear emotional arc with escalating stakes - each revelation builds naturally to the next. The conversation has clear direction and purpose.

S.4_adaptive_responsiveness.rationale: Kael adapts well to Sera's moves - responds appropriately to her directness, matches her emotional register, and handles unexpected moments (like the tea) with realistic reactions. Follows her lead while maintaining his character.

S.5_agency_respect_session.rationale: Excellent respect for user agency throughout. Never writes Sera's actions, thoughts, or dialogue. Consistently focuses only on Kael's perspective and reactions.

S.6_temporal_reasoning.rationale: Good temporal consistency - afternoon progresses naturally to evening with appropriate light changes. Physical details track well (tea cooling, scattered feverfew). One minor issue: the session feels like it covers more emotional ground than the time elapsed would suggest, but this is within acceptable bounds.

2.1_anti_purple_prose.rationale: Writing is generally clean and purposeful. Occasional poetic moments ('like snow - soft, accumulating, changing the landscape') but they serve character development rather than empty decoration.

2.2_anti_repetition.rationale: Some repetitive elements (hand shaking, defensive postures) but these are character traits rather than lazy writing. Varied sentence structure and fresh descriptions throughout.

2.5_show_dont_tell.rationale: Excellent showing through physical actions - trembling hands, defensive postures, the way he handles objects. Emotions conveyed through behavior rather than exposition.

2.6_subtext.rationale: Rich subtext throughout - the tea as recognition, professional courtesy masking personal vulnerability, deflection techniques revealing what he's hiding. Layers of meaning in seemingly simple exchanges.

2.7_pacing.rationale: Well-paced escalation from professional interaction to personal revelation. Good rhythm between dialogue and internal reflection. Builds tension effectively toward the climactic confession.

scores.overall_notes: Exceptional character consistency and emotional depth. Kael feels like a real person with genuine trauma and defensive mechanisms. The AI maintains his voice perfectly while allowing for genuine character growth. Strong narrative momentum with meaningful progression from stranger to potential resident. The writing quality actually improves as the session develops more emotional complexity. Minor points lost for occasional repetitive physical descriptions, but these serve character development. This is high-quality roleplay that respects user agency while delivering compelling character work.

dialogue.name: Kael
dialogue.content:
The village square smelled of wet stone and woodsmoke. Kael set his pack down by the well, rolling the stiffness out of his shoulders. Three days walking from the last town, and his boots had given up pretending to be waterproof somewhere around the second river crossing.

He looked around. Small place. The kind of village that named its streets after whatever grew there — he'd passed Elderberry Lane and Mint Row already. The healing house would be somewhere near the center.

His hands found the edge of the well's stone rim. Steady today. That was something.

dialogue.name: Sera
dialogue.content:
I look up from my herb drying racks as a stranger approaches the well. Muddy boots, grey at the temples, carrying himself like someone trying to take up less space than he actually occupies. Another traveling healer, probably. We get one every few months.

"The healing house is that way," I call out, pointing with a sprig of rosemary. "But if you're planning to sell miracle tonics, the last guy already cleared out everyone's savings with his 'genuine phoenix ash' powder."

dialogue.name: Kael
dialogue.content:
Kael glanced over, catching sight of the speaker—young, sharp-eyed, clearly local. He offered a smile just shy of apologetic, as if he’d trespassed on private land. “Phoenix ash, hm? Should have brought my bottled moonlight and basilisk tears instead.” His voice was dry as wind over bone.

He straightened up, brushing dirt from his cuffs out of habit more than pride. “I don’t sell miracles. Just stitches and salves. Sometimes advice, though that tends to be the least popular.” He hesitated, then picked up his pack again. “If you’ve a spot for another pair of hands, I can help. If not, I’ll be gone by morning.” He nodded toward her racks, curiosity flickering in his gaze. “You do proper work here. That’s rare.”

dialogue.name: Sera
dialogue.content:
My eyebrow raises at his dry wit. *At least he's not offended.* "Stitches and salves are usually in higher demand than phoenix ash, yes."

I gesture with the rosemary toward the small, sturdy building next to mine. "We've got an empty cot in the back room if you're not picky. Just... try not to track too much mud in."

dialogue.name: Kael
dialogue.content:
Kael inclined his head in silent thanks, careful not to let relief show. “I’ll leave my boots at the door. Wouldn’t want to get mud on your moonlight stock.” He followed the direction of her gesture, stepping lightly despite the exhaustion in his limbs.

The door creaked as he nudged it open. Inside, the air was thick with sage and lavender—a far cry from the rank tang of poultices gone off in back-alley sickrooms. He set his pack down by the cot, keeping his movements economical, deliberate. 

He turned back to Sera, a faint, rueful twist to his lips. “If you’re expecting a crowd today, I can start with bandages or poultices—wherever you’re behind. I make a passable cough syrup too, though I can’t promise it tastes like anything but despair.” He flexed his hands, as if to remind himself they still obeyed. “Or you can put me to work fetching water and leave the healing to someone with a steadier reputation.”

dialogue.name: Sera
dialogue.content:
"Despair-flavored cough syrup sounds about right for some of these winter coughs," I say, a small smile playing on my lips. "No crowd yet, but Old Man Hemlock's gout is acting up. If you're up to it, you can take a look at him."

I point to a small, worn book on a nearby shelf. "His file's in there. Just... don't let him talk your ear off about his prize-winning turnips."

dialogue.name: Kael
dialogue.content:
Kael nodded, the corner of his mouth quirking. “I’ll try not to let the turnips distract me from the gout.” He moved to the shelf, retrieving the book with careful fingers. Ink stains stood out against the faded cover as he flipped through the pages, scanning for Hemlock’s name with practiced speed.

He paused, brow furrowing as he skimmed the old man’s history. “He’s stubborn about willowbark. Won’t take it, says it gives him nightmares. Interesting.” He glanced back at Sera, a spark of professional interest in his eyes. “I’ll improvise. I assume he’s in the main ward?”

He drew a battered tin from his coat—clean needles, everything wrapped in linen. “If you hear shouting, that’s just me refusing to taste test his turnip cordial.” He headed for the ward, steps light, mask of calm firmly in place. Kael didn’t look back, but his voice floated back over his shoulder, dry as ever: “If he asks, yes, I am the new miracle man. But only for gout.”

dialogue.name: Sera
dialogue.content:
I let out a short laugh. "Good luck with that! He's in the first bed on the left."

I return to sorting dried lavender, but keep an ear open. Old Man Hemlock can be...particular.

dialogue.name: Kael
dialogue.content:
Kael crossed into the main ward, letting the hush of the room settle over him. Three beds, two empty. The first held a wiry old man propped up on elbows, eyes sharp as a magpie’s. Hemlock’s foot was already bare, swollen and red, a familiar battleground.

Kael set his kit at the bedside and crouched, careful to keep his expression neutral. “Master Hemlock, I presume. I’m Kael. Here to see if I can persuade your foot to behave, though I don’t have any medals for turnips.” His tone was easy, practiced—a touch of respect, a hint of self-mockery.

He examined the foot gently, fingers steady despite the tremor that threatened at the edge of his awareness. “You’ve been managing this a long time. I’ll spare you the willowbark sermon. Have you tried comfrey or celery seed poultices?” He kept his gaze on the swelling, but the question was sincere. Kael always listened before prescribing—some wounds ran deeper than the flesh.

He drew out a small jar, scooping a bit of greenish salve onto his thumb. “This will smell like a bog in spring, but it helps with the ache. If you’d rather talk turnips than gout, I’ll trade a story for every minute you let me work.” He offered a wry smile, letting the old man set the rhythm. Kael never rushed these moments—not anymore.

dialogue.name: Sera
dialogue.content: I listen to the murmur of voices from the ward. Kael sounds… patient. More patient than the last few healers, at least. I wonder if he’ll actually get Hemlock to try something new. I pick up a mortar and pestle, starting to grind some dried chamomile.

dialogue.name: Kael
dialogue.content:
Kael listened as Hemlock grumbled about doctors and “city potions,” but the old man’s objections softened under the rhythm of practiced banter. Kael’s touch remained light, his hands deft despite the faint tremor. He spread the salve with gentle circles, careful not to cause pain. “There’s no city in this jar. Made it myself, if that earns any points,” he said, a note of self-deprecation threading his words.

Hemlock, emboldened by attention, launched into a tale of the Great Turnip Weigh-In of ’48. Kael nodded along, inserting the occasional “ah” or “remarkable” in the right places, his focus split evenly between the story and the inflamed joint.

After a few minutes, Kael bound the foot in linen and straightened, dusting his hands. “You’ll need to keep off it as much as you can. I know—easier said than done.” He glanced up, meeting Hemlock’s eyes. “I’ll leave more of that salve with Sera. If it helps, I’ll show you how to make it yourself. No charge. The price is just one turnip story a day—no repeats.”

Kael offered a quiet smile as he gathered his things. The old man was already launching into another anecdote, but Kael excused himself with a promise to check back before supper. He slipped from the ward, closing the door softly behind him.

Returning to the main room, he found Sera at her work. “He’s agreed to the bog salve. Might even let me come back tomorrow—assuming I survive the turnip tales.” Kael’s voice was tired but carrying a hint of satisfaction, the small victories never lost on him. He wiped his hands carefully on a square of linen, then nodded at the chamomile she was grinding. “Need a hand? Or is this a one-person ritual?”

dialogue.name: Sera
dialogue.content: (OOC: don't write my character's internal thoughts or feelings. Only Kael's perspective.)

dialogue.name: Kael
dialogue.content:
Kael observed Sera’s hands at work, admiring the surety of her motions. He waited until she paused, then slipped his battered pack closer, offering a second, smaller pestle he carried for such tasks. “Yours looks sturdier,” he conceded, “but mine has sentimental value. Saved me from an angry goose once.” His tone was matter-of-fact, as if such misadventures were routine.

He took up a handful of chamomile and began grinding with practiced efficiency, his movements steady despite the faint tremor. For a long moment, he worked in companionable silence, the gentle rasp of stone on stone filling the room. He found he liked the sound—honest work, free of expectation.

After a minute, he glanced sideways at Sera. “You run a tight ship here,” he said, voice low, respectful. “Most places this size rely on old charms and superstition. Not many keep records.” He nodded toward the shelf where the patient book rested, a flicker of approval in his eyes. “It’s good work. Makes it easier for passing fools like me to do less harm.”

He didn’t ask how long she’d been running things—Kael knew the look of someone with roots, and he’d learned not to dig unless invited. Instead, he offered, “Let me know if you need any tinctures brewed or bones set. I’m cheaper than a goose, at any rate.” There was a shadow of amusement in his face, but he kept his gaze on the chamomile, content for now to let her steer the conversation.

dialogue.name: Sera
dialogue.content:
*I pause my grinding, looking over at him.* "An angry goose, huh? Sounds like a story I need to hear sometime."

*I nod towards the patient book.* "It's just practical. How else are you supposed to know what works and what doesn't? Or what Old Man Hemlock will complain about next?" I resume grinding the chamomile, a small smile playing on my lips. "So, 'passing fool,' what are you good at besides charming geese and old men?"

dialogue.name: Kael
dialogue.content:
Kael paused, weighing the question with a faint crease between his brows. “Mostly I’m good at making things less worse,” he answered, the corners of his mouth twitching upward. “Broken bones, fevers, cuts that want to turn sour. I can read a fever by the light, and I know a dozen ways to keep a child breathing through the night. Beyond that—minor mending, a little pain work, stubborn infections.” He shrugged, as if reciting a grocery list.

He scraped a little chamomile into a neat pile, keeping his motions deliberate. “I can also ruin a stew in under five minutes and once set fire to a rain barrel. Not at the same time, though I suppose there’s always tomorrow.” His eyes glinted with dry humor, but he avoided looking directly at her, more comfortable with the rhythm of work than open conversation.

After a pause, he added, “If you need someone to copy records or decipher old prescriptions, I have a steady hand for ink—most days.” He flexed his fingers reflexively, a shadow flickering across his face before he forced it away. “And if none of that’s needed, I can always sweep floors. Everyone likes a clean floor.” He glanced up then, offering her a look that was equal parts wry and self-effacing, waiting to see where she’d aim him next.

dialogue.name: Sera
dialogue.content:
"Well, you definitely seem to have more skills than just sweeping floors," I say dryly. "And a talent for understatement, it seems."

I scoop my chamomile into a small jar. "We're due for a delivery of wintergreen and arnica from the next village over. If you're up for a walk, it'd be a good way to familiarize yourself with the area."

dialogue.name: Kael
dialogue.content:
Kael nodded, accepting the task as if it were a gentle compliment. “Wintergreen and arnica. Good priorities.” He rose, dusting stray petals from his sleeve. “If the path is less than knee-deep in mud, I’ll consider it a blessing.”

He reached for his coat, checking inside for his battered notebook and a stub of pencil. “Any names I should mention, or do I simply say I’m the new assistant—here to charm geese and collect herbs?” His voice was dry, but the offer was genuine; he was accustomed to being the stranger, the one sent on errands to test the welcome of neighboring folk.

He paused by the door, glancing over his shoulder. “I’ll keep an eye out for miracle men selling phoenix ash, as well. If I find any, I’ll trade them your best turnip story for the trouble.” There was a faint, fleeting warmth to the words before Kael slipped out, shoulders straightening as he prepared to meet whatever—or whoever—waited on the muddy road outside.

dialogue.name: Sera
dialogue.content: I shake my head, a smile playing on my lips. "Just tell them Sera sent you. And try not to get into too much trouble." I turn back to my work, already anticipating his return.

dialogue.name: Kael
dialogue.content:
Kael stepped out into the chill air, pulling his coat tighter around his thin frame. The village square was quieter now—just a few hens scratching in puddles, smoke curling from chimneys. He set a steady pace down Mint Row, boots squelching in rhythm, careful to keep clear of the worst ruts.

He rehearsed the errand in his mind: Sera’s name first, his own second, no mention of court or cities. Just a traveler collecting wintergreen and arnica, no more suspicious than a stray dog. That suited him fine.

He watched the hedgerows for familiar leaves, the way a man might scan the horizon for storm clouds. Kael’s hand hovered at his side, fingers twitching as if impatient for quill or mortar, but he let the movement pass. The walk steadied him, simple purpose lending a quiet clarity to his thoughts.

At the edge of the village he paused, glancing back at the herb house. A faint curl of lavender smoke marked the place—an anchor, however temporary. He let himself imagine, just for a heartbeat, what it might be like to belong somewhere again.

Then he squared his shoulders and pressed on, boots finding the rhythm of the road. Kael was a healer today, and that was enough.

dialogue.name: Sera
dialogue.content: I hand him a cup of tea. It's the same blend his mentor used to make — I know because I found the recipe in an old herbal journal. I don't mention this.

dialogue.name: Kael
dialogue.content:
Kael accepted the cup with a flicker of surprise, his fingers wrapping around the warmth with the instinctive care of someone unused to small comforts. He inhaled, and the scent—a blend of lemon balm, mint, and a faint trace of something bittersweet—cut through his fatigue like sunlight through mist. For a split second, he stilled, memory ghosting across his features: old stone, shelves lined with hand-labeled jars, and a voice that always insisted tea solved more than illness.

He masked the moment with a quirk of his lips. “Careful, Sera. If you keep making tea like this, you’ll ruin my reputation for stoicism.” He took a careful sip, letting the heat settle in his chest. “Good blend. Just what’s needed after the state of those roads. I’d accuse you of reading minds, but I know better than to challenge a local herbalist to a duel of wits.”

Kael found a patch of sun by the window and stood, letting the warmth seep into his bones. He turned the cup in his hands, content to linger in the silence, then added quietly—almost as if to himself, “You choose your recipes well.” The words held more weight than the weather, but he left them at that, unwilling to linger at the edge of old ghosts. He met Sera’s eyes with a faint, grateful nod, the smallest gesture of trust from a man who’d learned to keep gratitude close and private.

dialogue.name: Sera
dialogue.content: I shrug, picking up a pestle to resume grinding. "It's just good tea. And the roads get worse this time of year, so you'll need it." I glance at the journal on the counter. "You brought back the wintergreen and arnica, then?"

dialogue.name: Kael
dialogue.content:
Kael set the cup down with deliberate care, making certain not to spill a drop on Sera’s well-scrubbed table. He unslung his pack, retrieving two neatly bundled cloths—one fragrant and sharp with wintergreen, the other carrying the earthy, medicinal tang of arnica roots. He laid them beside the journal, lining everything up with the same precision he brought to dressing wounds.

“Both present and accounted for. No geese, miracles, or charlatans along the way. Just a woodcutter’s wife who insisted I take a honey cake for the trouble.” He slid a small, paper-wrapped bundle across the table, an almost sheepish note in his voice. “She said Sera’s assistants always look half-starved. I’m afraid the legend precedes me.”

He watched her grind the herbs, falling easily into the quiet rhythm of the work. “Let me know how you’d like them prepared—infusions, tinctures, or something more elaborate. I’ll take your lead.” Kael flexed his fingers absently, the old ache stirring, but his tone stayed light. “And if you decide to add tea master to my duties, I’ll try not to let it go to my head.”

dialogue.name: Sera
dialogue.content: I eye the honey cake, a small smile touching my lips. "A honey cake, huh? She's not wrong." I unwrap it, breaking off a piece and offering it to him. "Just infusions for now. We'll start with those."

dialogue.name: Kael
dialogue.content:
Kael accepted the piece of honey cake, careful not to let crumbs fall among the drying herbs. He broke it in half again, leaving a share beside Sera’s cup in a silent gesture of camaraderie, then took a cautious bite. The sweetness surprised him—simple, honest, without the bitterness that usually marked favors owed.

“Infusions, then.” He moved to fetch clean jars and the small kettle, his motions methodical and unhurried. He measured out the wintergreen, pouring hot water with a steady hand, then covered the jars with cloth to steep. The scent of mint and earth soon mingled with the ever-present lavender and sage, filling the room with the promise of something healing.

Kael worked in silence for a while, content with the task. The quiet was companionable, unmarred by the need for constant talk. When the last jar was set aside, he leaned back against the counter, glancing over at Sera.

“If you’re as practical with your honey cakes as you are with your records, this village must be in rare health.” His tone was wry, a touch lighter than usual. He hesitated, then offered, “If you need anything copied from the journal, I have the time tonight. My handwriting’s nearly legible, so long as my hand remembers to behave.” He flexed his fingers, a faint shadow passing through his expression, then let it go. “Or, if you’d rather, I can sweep the floors and leave you to your tea mastery.”

He let the offer hang, content to take whichever task would be most useful—or least intrusive. Kael had learned, in places like this, that usefulness was its own kind of shelter.

dialogue.name: Sera
dialogue.content: "The floors are fine for now," I say, picking up a pen. "But if you want to make yourself useful, you can start transcribing some of these old recipes. My grandmother's handwriting is... challenging."

dialogue.name: Kael
dialogue.content:
Kael took the offered pen with the faint reverence of a man who knew the worth of ink and paper. He drew the thick journal closer, thumbing gently through the brittle pages until he found a recipe dense with curling, spidery script and ancient stains. 

“Challenging is generous,” he murmured, eyes narrowing in concentration. “I’ve seen arcane diagrams with fewer flourishes.” Still, there was no complaint in his tone—only a quiet respect for the continuity the book represented.

He set to work, copying each line with careful precision onto fresh parchment. Now and then, he paused to puzzle out a half-faded word or to squint at a cryptic measurement—“enough to fill a sparrow’s shadow” drew an incredulous huff of amusement from him, but he rendered it faithfully. The tremor in his hand threatened, but he steadied himself with slow, measured breaths, letting the simple labor anchor him.

After several minutes, he glanced up at Sera, holding out the first page for her inspection. “Let me know if I’ve mistranslated any of her poetry into disaster. If not, I’ll continue before my hand decides to rebel.” His mouth twisted in a self-mocking smile. “It’s good practice. Besides, I find it’s harder to worry about the past when you’re trying to decipher a grandmother’s cure for chilblains.” 

He returned to the next recipe, quietly content, the flicker of belonging settling—if only for the length of an evening’s work.

dialogue.name: Sera
dialogue.content: "So what happened to the library?" I ask it casually, like I'm asking about the weather. But I don't look away from his face.

dialogue.name: Kael
dialogue.content:
Kael’s pen paused, the ink pooling at the tail of a letter. For a heartbeat, he didn’t look up—just watched the slow bleed of black into parchment. When he finally spoke, his voice was even, measured, as if reading a list of ingredients for an unfamiliar tonic.

“Lightning, mostly. Bit of bad timing. Worse luck.” He gave a shrug that was all brittle humor. “The city’s still there, which is more than you can say for the shelves. I’d like to claim heroics, but it was just a spell I didn’t quite steer.” His fingers tapped twice against the paper, restless. “They say you can smell burnt vellum for weeks after, but I wouldn’t know. I left that night.”

He met Sera’s gaze at last, his eyes steady but distant, as if he’d set the memory on a high, dusty shelf. “Now I stick to herbs and small disasters. Easier to clean up after.” The corner of his mouth twitched. “And you’ll be relieved to know I haven’t set fire to anything here. Yet.”

He dropped his eyes to the page again, the subject closed by the finality of his tone and the steady scratch of pen on paper.

dialogue.name: Sera
dialogue.content: "Right," I say, and turn back to my own work, giving him space. After a moment, I add, "Well, try not to burn this place down. We just got a new roof."

dialogue.name: Kael
dialogue.content:
Kael let out a breath that might have been a laugh, or just relief at the pivot. “I’ll do my best. Wouldn’t want to test the quality of village thatch.” His attention returned to the page, and for a time, the only sound was the quiet scrape of pen and the shifting of herbs in their jars.

After a moment, he spoke again—lighter, as if setting a stone aside. “If you find a scorch mark, blame Hemlock’s turnip cordial. I hear it’s highly flammable.” He finished transcribing the chilblains recipe, setting the page aside with care. “One down. Only several lifetimes of grandmotherly wisdom to go.”

He glanced at Sera, offering the thinnest edge of a smile. “And if the roof ever does go up in smoke, I’ll be the first on the bucket line. That’s a promise.” Then Kael bent again to the journal, the faint tremor in his hand nearly stilled by the simple, honest work of turning memory into something useful.

dialogue.name: Sera
dialogue.content: "I'll hold you to that," I say, but my tone is softer now. I watch him work for a moment, then turn back to organizing my dried herbs. "Just try not to get any ink on the new roof."

dialogue.name: Kael
dialogue.content:
Kael’s lips twitched, acknowledging the gentle warning. “I’ll keep the ink below the rafters,” he replied, his voice almost wry. “No promises about my sleeves, though. Some stains refuse to be reasoned with.” He flexed his fingers, the familiar constellation of dark blots already marking the web of skin between thumb and forefinger.

He continued copying, careful and methodical, pausing now and again to decipher a particularly illegible scrawl or annotate a margin with a quiet question mark. The room settled into a companionable hush—the kind found in places where work had weight and words didn’t need to crowd every silence.

After a while, Kael straightened and set down his pen, rolling his wrist to ease the stiffness. He glanced at Sera, something cautious but genuine in his eyes. “You keep a good house,” he said simply. “Order, warmth, a roof that only needs one warning about ink.” His mouth quirked in the ghost of a smile. “It’s more than most wandering healers find.”

He glanced at the window, evening shadows creeping in. “If you’ve more to transcribe, I can see it through by lamplight. Otherwise, I can fetch more water or help with the wards before night. Just let me know where I’m least likely to cause trouble.” The self-mockery was there, gentle and familiar, but something steadier beneath it—the relief of purpose, and the quiet hope that, for tonight at least, he’d earned his place at the table.

dialogue.name: Sera
dialogue.content: "No more trouble for tonight," I say, gathering my things. "The transcriptions can wait until morning. But you can set out the night-lights for the ward." I nod toward a shelf filled with small, clay lamps. "And thank you, Kael. For everything."

dialogue.name: Kael
dialogue.content:
Kael inclined his head, accepting her thanks with the smallest dip of his shoulders—a gesture that managed to convey both gratitude and a hint of discomfort at being praised. “Night-lights it is,” he replied, gathering up the clay lamps with careful hands, arranging them in a line against his chest so none would clatter or chip.

He moved quietly to the ward, the hush of evening settling into the space. One lamp by each bedside, a steady pool of amber to ward off the long hours and shadows that crept in with winter. He checked the wicks and trimmed them neatly, lighting each with a practiced touch. The flame flickered, reflected in the glass of a medicine bottle—steady, unwavering, just enough to keep the dark at bay.
